Fern Por-rrrcAt- Pnecrrcss C oMMrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4_232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
February 6,2009
Jed Chernabaeff
Board Representative
Tulare County Board of Supervisors
2800 W. Burrel Ave.
Visalia, CA 93291
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-022
Dear Mr. Chemabaeff:
This letter is in response to your,request for advice regarding the mass mailing provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Because your question is leneral in nature, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.2
Please note, our advice is limited to the provisions of the Act, and we cannot advise you
with respect to whether the city-financed newsletter potentially implicates Government Code
Sections 8314 and 54964, and Penal Code Section424 -- areas of law outside the Act. We can
only advise you regarding the reporting provisions of the Act. However, you may wish to get
advice regarding this issue from your county counsel or the Office of the itto-"y General.
QIIESTIONS AND DTSCUSSTON
The Tulare County Board of Supervisors is exploring the idea of distributing a newsletter
by electronic mail. The purpose of the newsletter would be to highlight Board of Sirpervisor
activities, such as notable meeting decisions, updates on special pro.iects and commitiees,
employee recognition events, profiles and updater on ea.li Supervisor, and updates on important
county deadlines. You ask:
'The Politi.rl Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
' Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 831 I4; Regulation 19329(c)(3), copy enclosed..)


Our File No. I-09-022
PageZ
l' May the Board of Supervisors properly issue and send an electronic newsletter?
Section 89001 provides that "no newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense." Regulation 18901 clarifies that the prohibition onty ap"pties to items sent,
meaning delivered by any means to the recipient at his or her residen"", pru"" of employment or
business, or post office b-ox. For purposes or *ris subdivision (aXl), the item delivered to the
recipient must be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.
(Regulation 18901(a)(l).) Because distribution over the Intemet is not distribution of a tangible
item, it is not a prohibited mass mailing under the Act.
2' Are there any legal restrictions on the content of the electronic newsletter?
As noted above, the Act would not restrict the distribution of a newsletter on the lntemet.
while mailings that are subject to section 8900l and Regulation 18901 ur" fiorriuited when they
either: (A) feature an elected officer affiliated with the {"r"y which produces or sends the
mailing, or (B) include the name, office, photograph, o, ih", reference to an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or sendi the mailing, and is p."pu."a or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elicted officeq" that provision does
not apply to electronic mailings (Regulation lg90l(a)(2).)
However, you should be mindful that we cannot advise you with respect to possible
application of Government Code Sections 8314 and 54g64,andpenal Code secti on424,areas of
law outside the Act, which govern the appropriate use of public funds.
3' If yes, how can the Board ensure that hard copies of the newsletter do not become an
improper mass mailing?
we have advised that distribution of a publically funded item with the intent that it be
redistributed to a total of more than 200 additional persons at their residences, businesses, post
office boxes, or places of employment could result ln a violation of the mass mailing prohibition.
(Symkowicft Advice Letter, No. A-97-178.) In the Lavagetto Adviceletter, No. A-90-199, we
observed that an agency that sent its brochures to public"locations for pick up must take
reasonable precautions to prevent redistributio, of the brochures by a irrira pu.tv to the homes or
workplaces of more than 200 persons per month. In this case, the city may wish to expressly
limit redistribution of the newsletter in tangible form.


Our File No. I-09-022
page 3
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322_5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
Jwwjgl
Enclosure

sections.question: 1' May the Board of Supervisors properly issue and send an electronic newsletter? 2' Are there any legal restrictions on the content of the electronic newsletter? 3' If yes, how can the Board ensure that hard copies of the newsletter do not become an improper mass mailing?
sections.conclusion: Because distribution over the Internet is not distribution of a tangible item, it is not a prohibited mass mailing under the Act. As noted above, the Act would not restrict the distribution of a newsletter on the Internet. However, you should be mindful that we cannot advise you with respect to possible application of Government Code Sections 8314 and 54964, and Penal Code Section 424, areas of law outside the Act, which govern the appropriate use of public funds. If the city distributes hard copies, it may wish to expressly limit redistribution of the newsletter in tangible form.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Tulare County Board of Supervisors can distribute an electronic newsletter highlighting board activities and supervisor information, whether there are content restrictions on such an electronic newsletter, and how to prevent hard copies from violating mass mailing prohibitions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Electronic newsletter distribution over the Internet is not prohibited as a mass mailing under the Political Reform Act because it does not involve tangible items sent to residences or workplaces. Content restrictions for printed mailings do not apply to electronic distribution. To prevent hard copies from becoming an improper mass mailing, the agency should expressly limit redistribution of the newsletter in tangible form to avoid distribution to more than 200 persons.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document labeled as 'informal assistance' rather than formal advice letter; sections titled 'QUESTIONS AND DISCUSSION' rather than standard 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION'; informal advice does not provide immunity. Three distinct questions presented with corresponding discussion sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1' May the Board of Supervisors properly issue and send an electronic newsletter? 2' Are there any legal restrictions on the content of the electronic newsletter? 3' If yes, how can the Board ensure that hard copies of the newsletter do not become an improper mass mailing?

CONCLUSION: Because distribution over the Internet is not distribution of a tangible item, it is not a prohibited mass mailing under the Act. As noted above, the Act would not restrict the distribution of a newsletter on the Internet. However, you should be mindful that we cannot advise you with respect to possible application of Government Code Sections 8314 and 54964, and Penal Code Section 424, areas of law outside the Act, which govern the appropriate use of public funds. If the city distributes hard copies, it may wish to expressly limit redistribution of the newsletter in tangible form.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Tulare County Board of Supervisors may distribute an electronic newsletter highlighting board activities without violating mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act, but should be mindful of other state laws governing use of public funds and should restrict redistribution of any printed copies.
